source.title: Novelty and Dopaminergic Modulation of Memory Persistence: A Tale of Two Systems.

confidence_rationale: High-quality review in a top journal that explicitly synthesizes the LC vs VTA dual-system framework and maps these to distinct types of novelty (distinct vs common novelty) and distinct hippocampal memory operations (episodic vs semantic consolidation). Directly supports the MECH-075 bifurcation framing. Confidence modestly reduced because it is a review/opinion piece, not primary data.
failure_signatures: VTA dopamine responds to 'common novelty' (shares features with past experience) and promotes semantic memory via systems consolidation
LC dopamine responds to 'distinct novelty' (minimal overlap with past experience) and promotes strong episodic memory via local HPC consolidation
The two systems target distinct hippocampal circuits and operate on different temporal scales
Dopamine from both sources modulates memory persistence through D1/D5-dependent mechanisms
LC co-releases noradrenaline and dopamine; both are functional for memory in dorsal HPC

mapping.source_claim_statement: VTA and LC represent two distinct dopaminergic systems for hippocampal memory modulation: VTA handles common novelty and long-range semantic consolidation; LC handles distinct novelty and strong initial episodic encoding in HPC.
mapping.ree_translation: The MECH-075 bifurcation (dorsal=LC, ventral=VTA) maps onto functionally distinct roles: LC gating of dorsal HPC viability map corresponds to the encoding of genuinely novel situations (new map entries), while VTA gating of ventral HPC corresponds to affective/motivational valence assignment and semantic generalization of outcomes. Both arms are required for a complete REE viability map -- episodic entries via LC-dorsal and valence/generalization via VTA-ventral.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Review/opinion piece -- synthesizes existing data but generates novel theoretical claims not all directly tested. The 'common vs distinct novelty' distinction is a conceptual framework that organizes the literature but may oversimplify. The REE translation adds one more conceptual layer on top of an already theoretical paper.
mapping.source_context: Review article synthesizing animal and human studies on dopamine, novelty, and hippocampal memory consolidation; covers both LC and VTA literature

confidence_components.notes: Published in Trends in Neurosciences (high-impact review venue); primary data synthesis is robust. Mapping fidelity reduced because this is a theoretical framework being mapped to another theoretical framework (REE). Transfer risk moderate: the novelty taxonomy (common vs distinct) does not map cleanly to REE categories (new map entry vs valence update) without additional scaffolding.
